data.title: Cava: The Inevitable Slowdown Hammer
data.description:
Cava (NYSE:CAVA) was the hottest topic in the restaurant sector since its IPO, with the stock reaching valuation heights never seen in the sector.

The idea of buying "The Next Chipotle" (CMG) pushed analysts to do some interesting acrobatics to justify Cava's valuation. At the end of the day, as I've warned, the momentum had to stop, and Cava's industry-leading growth had to come down to earth at some point, as the initial halo effect begins to fade.

Investors are now looking at a stock in a 58% drawdown that still trades at a lofty valuation, and wondering what they should do. I argue that Cava is still too pricey here, and encourage patience.

Background

Cava went public in a June 2023 IPO with an actual public price of around $40, at a market cap of about $4.7 billion. I wrote at the time that Cava has the potential to become America's next big fast-casual chain, and yet, I thought the valuation was highly risky, reflecting the market's eagerness to participate in what could very well be "The Next Chipotle."

Little did I know this eagerness would go so far as to push the stock to almost triple in less than two years. Still, I continued to argue that valuation is very far ahead of fundamentals, and that chasing stocks that trade entirely on momentum is one of the worst risk/reward trades investors tend to do.

Ultimately, after this extraordinary run, investors who held since IPO are now underperforming the market, after a near-60% drawdown from the 2024 peak.

A selloff that started due to risk-taking and value normalization was then exacerbated by disappointing Q2 numbers and a guidance cut.

Reality Check In Second-Quarter Results

Results in the second quarter missed expectations, with revenue coming 3% below estimates, at $278 million. EPS did beat, but at this point, it isn't a main metric investors look at.

Revenue growth of 20.3% Y/Y was primarily driven by new openings, with operating weeks up 17.6% Y/Y. Same-restaurant sales growth was 2.1%, the lowest level since Cava went public, as traffic was flat for the first time since the brand's inception.

Cava achieved restaurant-margin levels that are considered exceptional for the sector, which is quite impressive on such a small footprint, but it's both a gift and a curse. On one hand, it reflects strong unit economics. On the other hand, there's no major margin expansion story to lean on, at the restaurant level, as Cava is already at the high end of the industry.

The weaker-than-expected quarter led management to cut its outlook for the year, now expecting same-restaurant growth in the range of 4%-6%, down from 6%-8%.

Meanwhile, they are turning to new sauces, drinks, and a recently announced Chick Shawarma protein in hopes of driving frequency and protecting growth.

Overall, this was precisely the type of quarter I was expecting would send shares into a downward spiral; it just came a little earlier than I expected. Results were fine, but very disappointing against lofty expectations.

Cava Is No Longer At The Top Of The Industry

In my last article covering Cava, I said that investors are making a mistake by assuming its remarkable same-store growth levels are sustainable. I specifically said that Cava's extraordinary numbers raise long-term questions as they are reflective of a "New Player" halo, rather than structural tailwinds.

It turns out that my prediction was correct, as Cava went down to No. 9 last quarter, the worst level since its IPO except for Q1-24, which lapped unusually tough comps.

This time, although comps were indeed tougher than the previous quarter, it seems that the slowdown is much more fundamental and can be attributed to normalization, as well as market share gains from value-oriented players.

It shouldn't be surprising that companies like Taco Bell (YUM), McDonald's (MCD), and Domino's (DPZ) are winning over the consumer with their value offerings.

Notably, so far in 2025, the "value players" are significantly outperforming the "quality players" like Chipotle and Cava. It also didn't help that the latter group used to trade at far richer valuations on the premise of market share gains, which have now stopped, or at least been suspended.

Valuation & The Chipotle Dream

At $64 a share, Cava still trades at unprecedented valuations in the sector, at 116 times forward earnings, and 95 times next year's estimates. On sales, Cava is at a place we have seen occasionally, but for companies that are much more profitable and at a more predictable stage in their growth journey.

For instance, Chipotle has traded above 5 times sales occasionally since 2020. It was generating double the margin, it had more than 10x the number of stores, and it had already proven it can maintain high-teens growth at a large scale. Also worth noting that shares didn't perform so well from those valuation peaks.

In investing, too often investors justify bad near-term decisions with the excuse that they are long-term investors. Buying shares with significant intrinsic risk just because they could perform well in the long term isn't rewarded. In fact, it's one of the biggest drivers of huge losses for investors.

When a company like Cava trades on the dream of becoming the next Chipotle, fulfilling which is more than a decade away, investors can rest assured they'll have plenty of opportunities to buy the stock at a better risk/reward set-up.

Such a setup isn't fully a matter of price. It's also a matter of certainty, predictability, and stability. Waiting to see where Cava settles after its halo effect fades was, and still is, in my mind, the highest expected-value decision.

Conclusion

The slowdown hammer finally came down on Cava, as the company is now facing a challenging yet important stage of normalization.

I fully expect Cava to come out strong from this stretch, as I view their unique menu and strong management as highly likely to succeed in the long term.

I still believe Cava will grow to thousands of locations in the United States, and yet, even after a near-60% slowdown, I don't like the upside I'm getting from investing at the current valuation.

Cava still trades at unprecedented valuation levels for the restaurant sector, and until I get an attractive entry point, I'm willing to risk significant near-term losses for a reward that's far into the future.

As such, I reiterate a "Hold" rating for Cava.

data.title: JETS: The Airline Industry Is Losing Its Spirit
data.description:
The airline industry has perhaps been the most systemically weak investment this century. Most of the major airlines are still trading in their 2000s-era trading range, having little in dividends to offset prolonged stagnation. I've covered many of the individual companies over the past year, but I last researched the entire industry in the 2021 article regarding U.S. Global Jets ETF (NYSEARCA:JETS) in "JETS: Skyrocketing Jet Fuel Costs May Upset The Rebound In Airlines." Despite over four years passing, seeing the S&P 500 (SP500) rise ~57%, JETS is down 3.4% since then, with a 2.7% total return loss.

JETS is a market-cap weighted fund of major airlines. Most of its largest holdings are U.S. airlines, though it has small exposure to some foreign airlines. Most of my views relate to the U.S. market, but many of the cost-side factors and economic demand trends are impacting all global airlines. JETS' expense ratio is reasonable at 60 bps, with a weighted-average "P/E" of 17X today. That is a slightly high valuation for the industry, driven by EPS compression due to margin pressures since ~2023.

Although my article focused on the impact of jet fuel, the thesis related more to the inflation fallout. To me, it was the combination of rising fuel, labor, and capital overhead costs. At the same time, demand was rising (and was stronger than I expected), but it was not strong enough to pass all of these costs forward.

The competitive landscape of airlines is immense, making the industry suffer from the economic "law" of the "zero-profit condition" (prices falling toward marginal costs). That theory rarely holds outside of companies that lack a competitive moat that differentiates them from others. Airlines increasingly are not differentiated, with most seeing weaker customer loyalty or regional advantages.

Today, there are some indications that competitive profit erosion is slowing. I think the likely liquidation or widespread closure of Spirit Airlines (OTC:SAVEQ) operations will decrease this pressure. This impacts fare pricing as well as competition for labor and physical capital. That said, we're seeing more negative demand trends and ongoing debt issues that could spur more declines.

Inflationary Risks Become Deflationary

When considering airlines, I think we must focus on the decades of weak performance. Though I have not measured it exactly, traders could have likely generated significant alpha by merely betting on mean-reversion, buying JETS when it's down and selling high. There have been relatively large short-term swings in JETS over the past decade, with the occasional large dislocation, as in early 2020. Jet fuel prices (a key to my last thesis) have some inverse influence (particularly in recent years), yet fuel is relatively cheap today:

The core of my bearish outlook was that airlines would see lower operating margins due to rising costs that could not be easily passed forward. To me, this issue, combined with excess leverage, is what fundamentally plagues airlines. For the past nine years, there's been a relatively linear negative trend in the largest airlines' operating margins. The margin collapse in 2020 dislocated that trend, but for the most part, we saw airline margins initially rebound to 2019 levels before returning to that negative long-term trend:

The change in margins generally tracks that of air fares. Airline fares, based on the CPI metric, have been down over the past decade, aligning with the somewhat linear negative margin trend. At the same time, demand for passenger travel, not limited to airlines (TSI index including rail), has plateaued below pre-COVID levels:

The lower peak in the passenger TSI index tells me that demand is insufficient to pass costs forward into fares. The strain has been most pronounced for low-cost airlines. Spirit, now twice bankrupt, had the worst margins, but all of the others have also struggled with very thin and depressed operating margins:

Low-cost airlines have struggled with low loyalty from both customers and employees. Increasingly, pilots will seek initial training and experience at "low-cost" airlines before taking higher-paying jobs at the "Big Three." Airline loyalty programs have offered less value than in the past, being one of the few ways airlines can create competitive moats. There is less room for profit with the rise of technological means for customers to select the cheapest ticket (Skyscanner, etc.) easily.

Now, fewer planes in the air should mean less competitive strain. Since late last year, I've predicted that Spirit will likely ultimately face liquidation because its (lower quality) assets (at that time) were not worth enough to offset its liabilities. The United Airlines (UAL) CEO recently agreed, saying that Spirit would most likely "go out of business" after ending service in numerous cities, opening the door to competitors like Frontier.

While lower ULCC competition, as well as the decline in (labor and fuel) inflationary pressures, alleviates the key issue we've seen since 2021. However, over the next year, I think airlines will face more pressure from demand. Even the market leader, Delta (DAL), is seeing its sales stagnate. Jet fuel production correlates to DAL's revenue, rebounding to peak 2019 levels, but not breaking a new high:

Delta's margins avoided much of the pressure faced by its peers over recent years due to the strength of its loyalty and business travel program. So, when it faces demand-side pressure, we can be relatively confident that the wider industry will too.

Thinking about the economy generally, I see bifurcation as the primary trend. Overall travel demand has weakened slightly this year, but premium cabin sales have continued to rise. The GDP per capita, an average income figure, has continued to rise, but median annual pay for job stayers has plateaued, falling compared to consumer prices. See below:

Personal savings levels are stuck below the pre-COVID range, consumer debts (and service cost ratios) are rising, and consumer sentiment is poor. Unemployment is not a critical issue, but hiring activity is low, with more people now looking for jobs than open jobs. In my view, that latter point strongly indicates continued unemployment increases, particularly if we consider many people have tapped out debt to offset less median income growth (and higher living costs), limiting future consumer demand (for median households).

Again, the issue I see in predicting the economic direction is bifurcation. Median consumer statistics are recessionary, but average data, which is skewed toward top brackets, are relatively strong. Along the same vein, the S&P 500, which is around 2X "normal" concentration in the top ten firms, has performed very well in recent years, but the small-cap Russell 2000 (IWM) is almost unchanged since 2021. The "economy" is growing, but that growth is very skewed toward a few sectors like healthcare.

In my view, these data are unsupportive to the airline industry unless airlines shift aggressively toward a premium-travel model. We're looking at the median household, still strained by higher living costs, post-COVID debt issues, and slower income growth (likely even more with low job openings). Thus, I doubt airlines can sell economy tickets for a profit, at least a material one. Assuming labor/wage deflationary pressures are growing, that issue should worsen over the next year.

Airline Valuations are Skewed by Debt

I often see online comments pointing to low stock prices as an indication of low valuation. However, a few airlines have never been more expensive if we account for their net debt. Particularly after their debt spike in 2020, most airlines have extremely high operating leverage, some facing issues with higher interest spending costs. Delta and United (UAL) are near peak enterprise values. American Airlines (AAL) and JetBlue (JBLU) have had awfully high post-COVID leverage, while others have worked to improve solvency since last year:

In my opinion, outside of the ULCCs, American Airlines may be at the highest risk of serious financial issues in a recessionary event due to its leverage and weak margin trend. Still, unless we assume an unlikely sharp turnaround in economic trends impacting airlines, I do not see any of these companies are undervalued.

The Bottom Line

Overall, I remain bearish on JETS. The airline industry is, to me, a systemically poor investment due to technologically-driven competition (weaker employee and customer loyalty) and, looking forward, to weak economic trends facing "economy class" travel.

That isn't to say all airlines are bad investments, or that it's worth betting against JETS. To me, most airlines, particularly the most highly levered ones, are likely overvalued because they may struggle to borrow money if margin pressures continue to mount. A rise in unemployment will accelerate this issue, but I do not expect margin pressures to alleviate until the airline industry significantly consolidates.

We're seeing Southwest (LUV) push into the "Big Three" domain as the low-cost carriers fight for the bottom of the market. This could happen through merger deals or through liquidations, but after the Spirit-Jet Blue failure, I lean more toward the latter. Of course, rate cuts may provide minor alleviation, particularly in the financing department; however, I do not think rate cuts will do much to improve economy-class travel demand, which seems to be the mounting issue today.

To me, the only way for JETS to turn around would be for both <3% inflation, rate cuts, and sustained high-breadth (not top-skewed) economy expansion. That is all possible, but seemingly unlikely based on data trends.

data.title: Super Micro Computer: The AI Comeback Story Of 2025 Has Just Begun
data.description:
Last time I wrote about Super Micro Computer, Inc. (NASDAQ:SMCI) stock was in early August, a few days before the company reported its fiscal Q4 2025 results, so I was generally just previewing the print and having a Buy rating on the stock at the time.

Unfortunately, SMCI corrected once the Q4 press release kicked in, as the firm reported a double-miss on both top and bottom line headline figures:

On the surface, indeed, the numbers were weaker than most bulls had expected (including my own previous inputs), and that's a logical outcome that the stock price corrected by over 14% on the release. But the whole pessimism in the report was coming from the past. I mean, the backward-looking metrics played a very important role in building the post-Q4 narrative on the Street, and I think that the market simply lost sight of what really matters for SMCI and its investors.

SMCI had a capital constraint that limited its ability to scale production, and as the CEO and the whole executive staff explained on the Q4 call, Super Micro's revenue recognition was delayed due to a specification change from a major new customer. And the capital constraint came from the delayed 10-K filing from the previous year, so it was a thing of the past, right? The firm is now reporting its filings right on time, as far as I can see it, and so there should be no capital constraint next fiscal year. And when it comes to the delayed revenue recognition, it basically meant that SMCI should get the revenues anyway - we just have to wait for the September and December quarters to see it in their financials.

It's the first half of fiscal 2026 for SMCI, so it's not a long wait for the bulls. The updated guidance said that the FY2026 revenues should be at least $33 billion, so even after a lot of positive upgrades were made to the consensus estimates of the FY2026, the current consensus is still failing to correctly reflect even the minimum of what's coming.

And while we're waiting now, the news broke a few hours ago that Super Micro "began volume shipments of Nvidia's (NVDA) Blackwell Ultra solutions", including its Plug-and-Play-ready HGX B300 systems and full GB300 NVL72 racks:

The whole transition from Nvidia's Hopper to Blackwell architecture was recently seen as a generational leap in chip performance that should undertake a massive new wave of investments and CapEx, and the one racks supplier that could deeply integrate within Nvidia's and Advanced Micro Devices' (AMD) offerings had always been sought after on the market. It's the reason we saw the rise of SMCI initially (its eventual fall was a completely different story that I think you probably already know if you've been following the stock for some time).

Anyway, the above news only proved to me that SMCI could now capture the most lucrative phase of the ongoing upgrade cycle, and it confirmed that the $33 billion sales the management said was their "least" target for FY2026 is actually doable. I know it sounds a bit odd, but that's how the market is supposed to work: Once it gets proof of an event it had thought was unlikely, it tries to immediately reprice the stock based on the new inputs. In the perfect world, the repricing process should indeed be immediate, and it's a part of the "efficient market hypothesis" (when an asset's price reflects all available information on the Street). But it seems like the initial pop of SMCI on the recently released news, which again, theoretically proves that those $33 billion in revenue is actually much more real rather than not, is evaporating.

Perhaps a lot of folks simply can't believe that SMCI will be able to achieve the margins needed for its EPS to fully recover, even with much higher sales. But I think if that's the market's concern, SMCI should be able to prove bears wrong in FY2026 (or at least by FY2027) as the strategic shift toward DCBBS (aka "Data Center Building Block Solution") and enterprise solutions should eventually steer the firm back toward its long-term gross margin target of 15-17%. The beneficial sales mix shift is taking place amid SMCI's plans to add 2-4 more large data center customers shortly (in addition to their existing base of four large customers).

In my most recent August take on Super Micro stock, I described it as cheap because it was trading at a discount to its historical multiples, and also the median values seen in its industry/niche.

I believe that SMCI should trade at ~25x [at least] by the end of calendar 2025, once the company shows that its margins do reverse and the order book does get monetized.

At the higher end of analysts' consensus estimates for FY 2026 EPS [$3.25], SMCI's price target arrives at $81.25/share, which is >30% above the current stock price.

As of today, nothing has changed.

SMCI is valued at less than 17x forward non-GAAP earnings, and while it's close to the stock's 5-year average value, the undervaluation relative to the sector's median is over 31%:

What I like to assess the most is the stock's forward PEG ratio, which shows the way the stock is valued when we're taking into account both the absolute P/E ratio and the EPS growth in the future period. The forward non-GAAP PEG for SMCI flashes an "extreme undervaluation" sign, as it's over 64% below the norm of the sector.

I'd honestly expect SMCI to at least try to climb back up to its previous local high, having the latest corporate news as tailwinds for the move-up. Last time I saw a fair value of above $80/share. But now let me be a bit more conservative and say that SMCI should first reach the nearest resistance zone at $62-68/share, which is about 43% above the current price, based on my technical analysis below:

Of course, all buyers - both long-term ones and speculators - should understand the risks of my Buy call. I may be wrong again on multiple fronts, and there's always a chance that SMCI's fundamentals will never reach their heights of the past. For example, if we assume that their gross profit margin just keeps sliding lower and lower over the next few quarters, the reasons for a repricing will be minimal. And the current catalyst won't lead to anything sustainable.

But for what it's worth, I think that SMCI is a great medium-term pick in the tech niche after the 60%+ off correction that took place since March 2024. Yes, there were fundamental reasons for such a dramatic price action, and I can't call it a simple overreaction. But now things are turning around, and it's visible in the recently released corporate news from the firm, as well as from the revenue projections the management made on the Q4 earnings call. Still, the market is not willing to believe SMCI (at least it's what I see happening). And in times like this, it often makes sense to be a contrarian.

I remain bullish on SMCI on this catalyst.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: Jobs Or Inflation - What's Driving The Fed's Next Move?
data.description:
By Mike Larson

In this episode of the MoneyShow MoneyMasters Podcast, Jim Bianco, president and macro strategist at Bianco Research, and Steve Sosnick, chief strategist at Interactive Brokers, discuss how a 911,000-job revision is altering the outlook for the labor market and Fed policy.

Transcript

Mike Larson: The deteriorating labor market, worries about inflation and tariffs, political turmoil overseas that's roiling government bonds, questions about the sustainability of the AI spending boom, not to mention a point-by-point recap of what happened to the price of Amazon (AMZN) shares from 1999 onward, and what it was like being on the trading floor during the 1987 stock market crash. This week's MoneyShow MoneyMasters Podcast covers a lot of ground with two of the best in the economic and market analysis business. You're going to want to hear what they have to say and what it means for your investments as we head into the fourth quarter of the year.

Joining me are Jim Bianco, President and Macro Strategist at Bianco Research; and Steve Sosnick, Chief Strategist at Interactive Brokers. Gentlemen, thank you both for taking some time out to chat.

I want to kind of start with the jobs picture. I mean, that's been the story that certainly after the August report, and the number relative to expectations being very weak, and now we've just gotten these benchmark revisions, 911,000 jobs taken away from the prior tally. What are your thoughts on where the labor market stands, starting with you, Jim, and what do you think it's going to mean for markets moving forward?

Jim Bianco: Well, there's two things about the labor market that need to be delineated and I don't think that many people are doing it. One, it's clear that the labor market has been slowing, especially this year, that revision number that you mentioned that came out this morning is for March of ‘23, or April of ‘24 to March of ‘25.

So that's data that's now a year-and-a-half old, and it won't get applied to the data until February of next year anyway. This is just a preliminary estimate. And these preliminary estimates do change and they can change quite a bit. But the labor market has been slowing and the jobs market has been weakening.

But I'll say to you that that's like the irrelevant number right now. And what is the more relevant number is the population growth of the country has been dramatically slowing in the last three to four months because of the closing of the border. That matters a lot. There's 160 million people that have jobs. There's 212 million people between the ages of 16 and 64, which is what they consider the workforce.

So what is the biggest driver of jobs in the United States? What's the population growth of the country? And right now, we don't know what the population growth of the country is. And we suspect that the population growth could be slowing dramatically and we've got estimates. Goldman Sachs is now saying that 41,000 jobs is all we need to make in order to meet the current needs of the country. There's other estimates by the St. Louis Fed that's down around 30,000. American Enterprise Institute is arguing that it might be down around 0.

So here's the uncomfortable question. Jobs market is slowing. We printed 22,000 jobs. Guess what? That's enough jobs. Fed doesn't need to cut rates. We're okay with 22,000 jobs if we have no population growth. And the reason I put it out that way is almost nobody's talking about the second half of the equation because for decades we never had to talk about the second half of the equation because we've never seen population gyrations like we have in the last few years. First, the big influx of people coming in, and now the dramatic slowing of people coming out.

Mike Larson: Okay. It's an interesting take because again, like you said, it's not something I've seen talked about all that much, but I want to pivot, Steve, to the Fed. I mean, obviously, we're – I'm meeting in about nine days now or eight days now as we're talking, we're going to get that announcement. A great futures market was somewhere in the realm or pricing in what, 100 bps and cuts maybe between now and early ‘26. Do you think that that's overdone? Do you think that's underdone? Do you think it's accurate? Kind of what is your thought on policy?

Steve Sosnick: I actually – I think it's possibly a little bit overdone in terms – because remember the market's been ahead of the Fed almost continuously. At this point, remember we came into 2024 expecting eight rate cuts. We got the equivalent of four. All year we've been expecting more rate cuts and they've yet to really materialize.

So this is – that's fairly typical. Two aspects of it. Jim's points about the labor market, the sort of the stasis being okay are important. Although it is interesting to note that people – older workers are leaving the workforce as well. I have a feeling that might have something to do with the fact that they can afford to do so because in many, arguably the stock market is helping them do that.

But also manufacturing jobs are lower. There's a lot of weakness underneath the number. So the labor market is certainly at best questionable. But the flip side is the other part of the dual mandate, stable prices, sort of lost in the shuffle, in the enthusiasm at Powell's Jackson Hole statement with him more or less pivoting from, tell me why I shouldn't raise interest, tell me why I should cut interest rates to, tell me why I shouldn't cut interest rates, which was – which is, of course, meaningful. But the Fed – the FOMC statement was changed basically to say that the inflation target is 2%, not something over two, not a two handle, it's two, and that's not where we're trending now. The last four core PCE numbers when taken out to 2 digits have all been to the upside.

We're going to learn from core – from PPI and CPI this week. And if that follows the trend, or even if they come in as expected, that puts us at an annualized rate over 2%. And I'm not necessarily talking about the 12-month number because that's 11 months of stale data. I'm talking more what the economist prefers three or four months – annualizing three or four months data.

And so I do think that the idea that the Fed is just going to be gung-ho about cutting rates might prove a little more difficult. And that's not necessarily a market-friendly aspect to it, because if the Fed is slow, that raises the stagflation-y type of questions.

I'm not a believer in 70s stagflation hitting right now, but it's stagflation-like kind of thing, persistent, relatively high prices, and not a great labor market. And then, of course, if we do need massive rate cuts, I always caution, be careful what you wish for because if you have an economy that's so bad that it needs massive rate cuts, that's not necessarily good for the earnings that drive stocks.

Mike Larson: Got it. Jim, I want to talk a little bit about the shape of the curve, what's happening in the dollar and the political markets interaction. I mean, how much of the steepening curve is just normalization? How much of it is – what's happening with the pressure on the Fed, the political pressure being brought by the administration? Any thoughts on what's happening there and how things are going to unfold in the coming months?

Jim Bianco: Yeah, let's start with the dollar. The dollar decoupled after Liberation Day in April, where it was falling irrespective of what was also happening, but that was very short-lived. By about May, it's recoupled with the bond market. And now what seems to drive the dollar more than anything else is if yields go down, or then the dollar goes down, if yields go up, then the dollar goes up and yields are going down. And that's why we've been seeing a weaker dollar.

So it's more explained by the more pedestrian type of thing that we're just seeing lower yields and expectations of lower yields. It's not the loss of American exceptionalism. Everybody's abandoning the dollar or anything like that. I think that that was a brief summer storm months ago, and that is long since past.

As far as the yield curve goes, it has been steepening, especially at the long end of the curve. The 10-year and 30-year have been steepening a lot more than say the five- and seven-year part of the yield curve. And it's largely been steepening on what Steve was talking about with the stagflation story. You want to cut rates and the market is unsure about inflation. The long end of the yield curve is going to go sideways to higher, and the front end of the yield curve is going to go down, and you're going to wind up with a steeper yield curve. And that's what we've been seeing. It's usually a steeper yield curve. All things being equal is associated with stronger nominal growth. Now I use the word nominal. That could either be stronger real growth or inflation or a little bit of the others.

Now one last thing when you talk about yields, I should mention foreign yields because they're going up right now. And what's interesting about that is they're going up in the developed – I'm talking about the developed world now. They're going up in the developed world. The Japanese, the BOJ’s hiking rates, they're going to hike rates again. Their long-term yields are going up.

The ECB and the Bank of England have been cutting rates for a year. They've been doing exactly what Trump wants the Fed to do. And their long-term yields are going higher as well and have been for most of the last year or so.

So the rate cuts haven't been working. Again, it's fear of inflation. Their inflation numbers are a little bit sticky, even though they've got more of a stagflation story than the U.S. because they've been actually printing some negative GDPs in Europe. Also, they've got huge budget deficits. And then we've got a new thing now. We've got political risk that's coming into the equation.

For the first time ever, maybe ever, it looks like French 10-year yields are – they’re within 1 basis point of Italian 10-year yields. France has never had a higher yield than Italy, and they're 1 basis point away from having that happen, and that's because there's tremendous political risk right now in France. Their government has fallen again. Their prime minister was kicked out that – Macron's going to have to appoint the fourth prime minister in the last year or so, so that the political risk is taking its toll.

Now, why did I bring that up? If Europe is going higher in yields, Japan is going higher in yields, all things being equal, it will be a negative for the U.S. It will drag the U.S. higher because developed country yields tend to move in the same direction, not always, but more times than not, they tend to move in the same direction.

Mike Larson: Got it. Steve, I want to pivot to you and talk about the equity markets here. I mean, this was supposed to be everybody was sort of wringing their hands. It's August, September, seasonally weak. You got to worry about and maybe take some risk off the table. Yeah, we've been hitting highs. I mean, U.S. markets are underperforming foreign markets, but in general, equities have shrugged off a lot of the threats. Why do you think that is? Do you think that's sustainable and kind of how are you looking at things as we get closer to the fourth quarter?

Steve Sosnick: Well, I mean to some extent, there's the wall of worry idea that we've been climbing and this market has been basically Spider-Man when it comes to the wall of worry, just shimmy right up that wall. But in – but overall also, I'm only a believer in seasonality up to a point. Yes, there – as the calendar starts to shift, I think it's natural for people to start to, at least, institutional investors, to think more about capital preservation rather than strictly on returns or add some risk aversion to the massive risk assumption that we've been seeing in markets overall.

So that's relatively normal. But while September is clearly the worst month of the year, when you look back over a 25-year period, there are four months that have averaged lower returns. I think it’s – I don't have it right in front of me. I think it's January, February, I believe it's June, and certainly September. And September is by far the worst. The other three are somewhere just slightly below 0. September’s average is about 1.5% lower. But here's the thing about even September. Out of those 25, 12 of them have been higher, 12 of the average returns. So your seasonality here is a coin flip at best. 12 out of 13, obviously, 12 out of 25, obviously, is just under 50%.

Now, last year with a 2% rally, we wiped out five straight bad Septembers and those clearly are sticking in people's minds. But it's not, I don't love seasonality because it's one of those things that works until it doesn't. I think it's a guide, but it is by no means a hard and fast rule.

There are other things to keep in mind. I think right now I'm hearing a bit more chatter about return of investment of money that's being spent on AI. If that becomes more of a theme, that could weigh on the markets because we're so dependent upon the AI trade. But that's really just a ground – sort of a groundswell and undercurrent at this point.

So I think so much more depends on our old friends, MOMO and FOMO, and what our friends at the FOMC choose to do in response to the various topics we've been discussing.

Mike Larson: The next question, I guess, is kind of for either of you who'd want to take this. I mean, you mentioned tech and AI and whether there might be some loss of momentum in that part of the market. I mean, I look at things like the yield curve doing what it's doing. You would maybe argue that might be powerful for financials as sort of a driver. What are your thoughts on -- from a sector standpoint, how things shake out, again, as we kind of get towards the tail end of the year and people start placing bets for ‘26.

Jim Bianco: Oh, there's a lot of ways you can go with that question when you're talking about tech. I'm always reminded of the famous Bill Gates quote from the late 1990s, which was so true and applies today, that any technological innovation over the next couple of years will deliver you less than what you expect. And over the next 10-plus years will deliver you more than what you expect. And I think that that totally applies when it comes to AI. That what all of this data center and infrastructure build out, the expectations of what we're going to get out of it in terms of profit productivity and the economy and the like is going to underwhelm us. It's going to be less than what we expect.

But over the next five to 10 years, I think that AI is going to be as revolutionary as the Internet itself, if not bigger. And I am one that thinks that I've read estimates that up to a third of jobs in the current economy could be affected by AI. And I think that that might be right. But I don't think that's going to happen immediately.

And so the big thing that the market has to deal with is these companies are going to be dumping hundreds of billions of dollars into AI investment and they ain't going to get anything out of it in ‘26 or ‘27 or ‘28. Maybe in 2035, it'll be a gigantic home run if they're the winner. But for the next couple of years, it's going to be very little. And I think that reality is starting to come into the market right now. And you just don't want to buy them all up because there's going to be winners and there's going to be losers.

You can look at all the big AI spenders, and you're going to also make the case that a couple of these are going to fail and a couple of these are going to be huge home runs. And which ones are they going to be? And they're spending gigantic sums of money in order to try and be the winners. And it's going to be real hard to figure out which ones are going to be the winners and which ones are going to be the losers.

Mike Larson: Steve, what would you add to that since you brought up the topic up a little bit ago?

Steve Sosnick: Sure. I mean, I think we're along the same lines here. I mean, for better or worse, we're all kind of veterans of the 1999, 2001 era. And I'll stipulate, as Jim raised the point, the Internet was everything that the bullish – the most bullish – the most exuberant futurists were saying about the potential of the Internet, it came true, but it didn't come true for several years.

Bill Gates was right on this one by saying in the late 90s. And a lot of the big winners, as we know from that era, did not prove to be big winners. And remember, of course, the most successful Internet companies of the present didn't exist then. Google didn't exist. Meta didn't exist. Microsoft was considered an also-ran. So some of these things, life has a way of changing. You think about the – I was – someone asked me recently about the Nifty Fifty stocks and those were such one-decision stocks like tech stocks that were going to lead forever like Polaroid and Kodak and Xerox and need I go on?

So the technology has a way of changing and it's very unpredictable. And so I think as I start to read more, there was the MIT study that only 5% of money spent by end users on AI has yet to pay off. There's another article I read in the Atlantic yesterday, just came out this week, that in an early study they asked coders how much productivity they thought AI was going to add. And one estimate was 40%, another was 20%.

The reality was negative 20% because AI, as of now, still requires humans to realize that where AI makes mistakes, and it does make mistakes. Will it make those same mistakes 10, 20 years from now? I doubt it. But – so this is part of it. So it's a transformation, but we've priced in the full transformation.

The other thing to keep in mind here is the capital spending that's going on in AI is a huge contributor to GDP. And the earnings from these companies, from the big winners so far, is – and the market capitalization of these companies is so – is such a huge percentage of the S&P 500 that were very susceptible and we – to a decline. And we did see a real-world example of this in April, when stocks fell, the bigger the stock, the worse it was. The Mag 7 was worse than NDX, which was worse than SPX, which was way worse than the equal weighted SPX.

So this is not to say the sky is falling, but if there's this rationalization of, at least, the questions being asked of how the – is all this money being spent? Is it going to keep increasing at this rate and how much is the market depending on that increased spending that can give us some bumps along the road and, I think, let's say from as a long-time options trader, I do have to wonder if volatility is – the volatility measures are really pricing that in. And it's not clear to me that they are right now.

Mike Larson: Got it.

Jim Bianco: If I could add to that just a couple of quick anecdotes to that. If you go back to ‘99, in 1999, Time Magazine's Person of the Year was Jeff Bezos in the coming Internet revolution. And as Steve said, if you took the most optimistic scenario that they painted in that story, the Internet exceeded it. It was way more optimistic than that, but here's the rub.

In ‘99, this is pre-split Amazon. Amazon stock was $100 in ‘99. By 2001, it was $6. You had lost 94% of your money if you had bought Amazon in ‘99 saying, wow, the Internet is going to be greater than anybody's wildest imagination. First, you lost 94% of your money. In 2010, it was still $10. You had lost 90% of your money for 11 years. Then Amazon went from $10 pre-split to $3,300. And I know I say this to people and it doesn't register with them. You'd lose 90% of your money for a decade and then you'd make 30x on your money.

Oh, I should have bought the stock. No, you wouldn't have bought the stock. You would have fired every manager that put you in that stock 6x over. This is the risk you face. It took the Internet longer than anybody thought, but once it finally got traction, it was way bigger than everybody thought. And that's where I think AI is going to be at the same time.

And I'll just reemphasize one other point that Steve said, too. I could remember back in ‘99, we were talking about Cisco (CSCO) being the first trillion-dollar company, in which, by the way, it never has been and we've got multiple trillion-dollar companies. And that's because that was they were making the switches and the routers for the Internet. They were the overused Wall Street term, the picks-and-shovels play, the hardware play.

It's never the hardware play that really makes you all the money. It's the content play. It's when you invented Google and eBay and Facebook, and what do you do with the Internet that's where you make the big money.

As far as AI goes, we're still in the infrastructure play. We're still buying NVIDIA (NVDA). We're buying everybody who makes the chips, makes the stuff for AI. What's coming is companies that don't exist yet. The content companies that say, now that we have AI, what are we going to do with it? Those are the companies that you're going to have to wait for. And that's when the realization of this technology will really come to fulfillment.

Mike Larson: Got it.

Steve Sosnick: If you don't mind, that was exactly going to be my next point was the best AI company is probably being developed in a garage somewhere, maybe in Silicon Valley, maybe in Kansas, maybe in Chongqing. I have no idea. But the point – that point is very important is, I think, the ultimate winner in AI is probably yet to be acknowledged or even yet to exist.

Mike Larson: Got it.

Jim Bianco: And that company though, they still might be in school, the guys that are going to develop that company.

Steve Sosnick: Oh, absolutely. They might be in gym class right now for all we know. Exactly.

Jim Bianco: Yeah.

Mike Larson: Well, I'd be remiss. I love the discussion of the super-high tech part of the market and the future forward part of what's going to happen down the road. But I have to just briefly pivot to one of the oldest industries, dirtiest industries out there, and that's things like mining, metals. We had another mega merger in the day, we're recording this $55 billion tie-up. Metals have been phenomenal performers.

What do you think about that part of the market? Do you think this is going to continue, or is this something that we're just getting too exuberant maybe again in that old-world industry at this point? And this is just for whomever wants to take it.

Steve Sosnick: I'll go on this one, if you don't mind, Jim. The – it's funny because I started my career trading international stocks, basically Canada, Australia. This was in the 80s. And so it was a big, late 80s, big mining, big mining boom. So these names have been familiar to me for my entire career and we've seen this come and go.

The rally in gold, I think, is very specific. And the reason I say that is, to all the central banking types of points Jim raised earlier in the discussion, I do think some of the rally in gold is the fact that you're seeing central banks take some – move some of their reserves into gold away from the dollar, and that's a much less liquid market than the dollar.

So if – it doesn't take as much to move it, and that's been providing a lot of the backdrop. Gold is always thought of as an inflation hedge. And it is if you live in a country that doesn't - that borrows in currencies other than its own. So the U.S., we can print more money. Japan, they can print more money.

If you live in Türkiye or Argentina or someplace like that, you really ought to hold some gold because they don’t have – they just can't print more money to pay their debts and they have these perennial currency crises and it's a way to diversify out of your home country.

So from a U.S. point of view, I view it as much as an anti-dollar as anything else. So I'm not refuting all that. I personally, I do have a percentage of my portfolio, always have had a percentage of my portfolio in gold as sort of a diversifier. It has been a bit of a decorrelator.

In terms of broader metals and mining and energy, that's more of a broad economic story. China has been the big push in terms of natural resources. Their economy, although they're trying to stimulate it, they're still in a bit of a deflationary cycle right now, which doesn't necessarily bode well. If the tariffs meaningfully hinder economic trade – hinder global trade, that doesn't help. So I do think that some of the more smokestack type of commodities do have some trouble here, even if gold has all the luster right now.

Mike Larson: Got it. Jim?

Jim Bianco: Yeah, I agree with the comments about gold is that it is kind of, I've always said that, everybody asks this generic question all the time. I'm uncomfortable with the financial system. How do I get my money out of the financial system?

Well, prior to crypto, which is a way you could do it, the only way you could do is gold. And you're not really all the way out. You're kind of only partially way out. So gold benefits when there's ever concern. One concern is inflation, but it's not the only concern. It could be deflation, it could be political risk and the like. And right now we've got a lot of political risk.

As I mentioned earlier, we've got tremendous political risk coming out of Europe right now. I think a lot of Americans do not realize how fraught the political risk is between Germany, the UK, France, that Italy is the beacon of stability in Europe. That's all you need to know about how bad it is in Europe, that you have to look to Italy to be your point of stability.

And then you've got, of course, the threat of independence with the Fed and tariffs and everything else. And it's no wonder that the instrument that you would associate with trying to get your money away from the financial system. Precious metals have been doing so well. And crypto being another way that you could do it, too, has been doing relatively well over the last year. So this scenario will probably continue as long as this instability is with us.

Mike Larson: Got it. Well, we're going to almost be out of time here. I just want to briefly get to each of you, Steve. You're going to be joining us in Orlando for the MoneyShow we have coming up there and your session is tentatively titled Happy 38th Birthday, Fed Put. Can you just let our listeners or viewers know, if they come to that session, what they're going to be learning about?

Steve Sosnick: Yeah, well, I like to say that I was there when – I was like in the maternity ward when the Fed Put was born, because I was on the trading desk at Salomon Brothers and it wasn't the day of the crash, it was actually the day after the crash, which for those of us who were around then, remember that that was actually worse at one point.

And what I like to remind people is that I think a lot of equity investors believe that the Fed Put is designed to protect the stock market. And my assertion has always been, it isn't. It's not about stocks. It's about the other unspoken aspects of the Fed's dual mandate, the stability of the banking system, the stability of the finance and payment system and all these other things.

And the economy overall, it does play into it. And so yes, that can help the stock market if the Fed gets active, but I think a lot of times people expect the Fed Put to be there when it isn't. And the question I always ask people is, tell me what the strike price is on the Fed Put and tell me, right? So that's the gist of it.

Mike Larson: Got it.

Jim Bianco: So can I just jump in there and say that, Steve, I love to see your presentation, because I was at Shearson Lehman Brothers a few blocks away during that same day in 1987 down in New York City. And you're absolutely right. It was that one sentence statement that Greenspan put out as the Fed Chairman, that the Fed stands ready, willing, and able to assist anybody under any circumstance that was really the Fed Put, because all the stocks had started closing and then MMI futures. Remember those, Steve? The MMI major market index futures reopened, limit up, and the market turned around that day and we've had a Fed Put ever since. I think you're absolutely right.

Steve Sosnick: It was more than the statement. It was more than the statement. It was basically, I don't want to go into the whole thing right now, but it was the Fed basically backstopped Salomon Brothers, Goldman Sachs, and Morgan Stanley and said, they – and then Stanley, Sachs - came out, cigar smoke emanating out of the room. And it was, I want all of you, and I'll leave some of the saltier language out, figure out what it would take to open your stocks that are basically offer only and just stick a bid out there for me, just put a bid out there.

We did it. Morgan Stanley did it. Goldman Sachs did it simultaneously. At the same time that Greenspan made that statement, that was all the market needed to – that was what the market did. We actually bought very little. We didn't buy nearly that much stock then, because just everybody – in those days on a floor, everybody shows up with that question, or NASDAQ where you – where everybody was just fleeing off the bids and just leaving them there and buying stuff, that was enough to get the market turned around. But boy, was that scary, because it really did feel like the financial system was going to come to a halt. And I don't hope we didn't go too far. I'll expand on this in our talk, Mike. Sorry, Jeff.

Jim Bianco: Yeah, Mike, I'll just say real quick. I think that that's an excellent topic because this event, the day after the stock market crash in 1987, is an extraordinarily important event in financial market history that needs to be appreciated.

Mike Larson: Well, listen, I mean, fascinating discussion here. We've covered a little bit of everything, market history, gold, stocks, AI, wonderful time. If you're watching this, viewers, the reason Steve and Jim are joining me here, both of them are going to be speaking at our upcoming events.

We've got Steve at the Orlando MoneyShow, which is set for October 16th to 18th at the Omni there in Southwest Orlando. And we're fortunate to have Jim joining us for the Sarasota Symposium Event. That's going to be December 1st to 3rd at the Ritz-Carlton here in downtown Sarasota.

Gentlemen, again, thank you so much for your time. Looking forward to talking and catching up with markets at those events.

Jim Bianco: Thank you.

Steve Sosnick: Thank you. Thank you, Mike. Looking forward to it.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: PHYS: Expect Some Near-Term Pressures, But Remain Focused On Long-Term Drivers
data.description:
Gold's spot price has been on a tear in recent months and with that the Sprott Physical Gold Trust (NYSEARCA:PHYS) has continued making new all-time highs.

The ETF achieved returns that are now comparable with those of the S&P 500 for the past 10-year period. What's quite unusual is that the equity market had one of its strongest years on record over that period and the supposedly lower risk - lower return precious metal has almost matched the cumulative annual growth rate of the equity index.

To illustrate that, PHYS annual return in the past 10 years stood at roughly 12%, which is above the long-term return profile of the equity market.

The precious metal bull market started all the way back in 2019, took a pause in 2021-22 period and has made an even stronger return after that. As one of the best ways to gain direct exposure to the price of gold, PHYS is up nearly 40% year-to-date and on track to achieve it best years on record.

Right of the bat, we have to establish a couple of characteristics about gold that some investors might be forgetting amidst this long-awaited bull market.

Firstly, the long-term thesis for gold is intact. I outlined it all the way back in 2019 and to put it briefly it relies on the need for a change of the current global monetary regime. Whether gradual or sudden, such change increases the attractiveness of monetary alternative like gold.

Secondly, keeping in mind everything said above, we should recognize that these are long-term trends. They do not occur over 1, 2 or 5 years. They could span over a decade or even more. That is why, short-term deviations from the long-term trend are quite common, especially when market participants are suddenly changing their minds about an asset class that has been shunned for many years.

Lastly, there is no way to accurately forecast the price of gold. As opposed to equities, where we could link the price to business fundamentals, the price of gold is a bit arbitrary. At one point most people believed that real interest rates were the best predictor of gold prices, but that narrative was quickly proven wrong in recent years.

The price of gold is no-longer related to real interest rates and the fears that higher rates would be a disaster for PHYS have been ill-informed.

Although predicting the price of gold is futile exercise, we could anticipate periods PHYS is likely to perform well and periods when the Trust's performance is likely to disappoint.

Using Leading Indicators As A Guidance

For this purpose we could take a look at the annual change of the Conference Board Leading Economic Index® (LEI). Since the Global Financial Crisis, there has been a tendency for either monetary or fiscal authorities to implement certain radical decisions when the annual change of the LEI turns negative and is signalling pending recession.

As we could see on the graph below, there are a number periods when the annual LEI change fell in negative territory to approached zero - 2012, 2016, 2019 and 2022 to today.

During the first period, in September of 2012, the Federal Reserve announced the beginning of its third round of quantitative easing, known as QE3. Thus enough liquidity was provided for a recession to be avoided. The price of gold did not react significantly to this event, but equities did.

The second period was in 2016 and around that time the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act was announced which provided enough support for the equity market. This time around, however, market participants begun to realize that monetary or fiscal intervention would probably be required every time a recession is on the horizon. Since this is a strong signal that the monetary regime is not working properly, the price of gold reacted strongly in early 2016 just to quickly cool-off by year end as risks subsided.

The next cycle begun in 2019, just prior to the pandemic. The yield curve begun steepening in mid-2019 and this sparked speculations that sooner or later more liquidity would be needed. This happened later on in 2020 when rates were cut and the Federal Reserve re-launched an aggressive quantitative easing program. This time around, however, it became crystal clear that the marked can not function properly without significant interventions from time to time and the gold price reacted accordingly.

The current downturn for LEI begun around 2022 and the risk of a recession has been exceptionally high for a number of years now. On top of the deteriorating leading indicators, we have also seen a relentless steepening of the yield curve

On top of all that, one of the biggest risks for the current monetary regime has been the growing fiscal deficit as a percentage of GDP. What's likely sparking most fear among market participants is the fact that budget deficit tends to widen significantly during recessions, but for the first time since World War 2 we are witnessing such high deficits during period of economic growth.

In my view, this has pushed the risk of a major change in the current monetary regime to all-time high and that is why we are witnessing the strong performance of PHYS over the past few years. The 110% return in just 3 years, however, is significantly higher than any performance we have seen recently which creates the necessary conditions for a temporary setback in gold prices.

Additionally, the risk of a market sell-off is now extremely high and such an event would trigger a negative reaction of PHYS as usually the initial reaction to market participants is to rush and sell all assets.

Conclusion

The long-term attractiveness of PHYS as an addition to an all-equity portfolio remains intact. Until the current monetary regime is restructured or an entirely new system is put in place, gold would remain a good way to reduce portfolio volatility and hedge some major risks for equities. By closely following the annual changes of LEI and its short-term cycles, investors could distinguish between periods when adding gold is likely to pay-off and when the most prudent thing to would be just hold. With that in mind, I believe that right now the risk of a short-term correction of PHYS is too high and a Hold rating is justified.

data.title: Wall Street Roundup: Red Flag, Green Flag
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Oracle jumps to a new high on earnings, up 85% YTD (0:30). Adobe down - is monetization keeping pace? (1:35) Broadcom, Nebius surge on customer acquisitions (3:00). LULU drops post-earnings; retail and tariffs (3:55). Jobs data weaker than thought (8:50). IPO market waking up (11:00). Momentous Fed meeting (14:55).

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Welcome to another week of Wall Street Roundup, by popular request now being recorded on a Friday morning after the jobs data.

Welcome to a Friday of Wall Street Roundup, Brian Stewart.

Brian Stewart: Great to be here. End of the week.

RS: End of the week. Always great to have you talking news even if it looks terrible outside our windows, spiritually speaking. Now what do you have on the agenda? We've had some tech earnings. We've had a bit of retail earnings. What are you mostly focused on as we say goodbye to another week?

BS: If we're looking at this week, I think the top story has to be Oracle (ORCL). The company jumped 76% to a new high following its earnings release.

It actually missed expectations for both earnings and revenue, but revealed four new major contracts. This represented a 359% surge in its remaining performance obligations, which are now sitting at about $455,000,000,000.

So the bet that a lot of people are making is just that the company's future looks really bright even if the last quarter was a bit of a meh.

The money that's been committed in terms of contract will eventually hit the bottom line, and so the company is going to see significant upside in the future.

RS: And what else would you add to the earnings conversation?

BS: I think Oracle is an interesting contrast to Adobe (ADBE). Adobe is down slightly now, after its earnings reported last night.

It had sort of the opposite situation. So it had a beaten raise in its most recent quarter. On the face of it, more impressive than Oracle, but the stock is just trailing off slightly.

Digging into the results, AI adoption has been pretty strong within the company, but there's some questions that analysts have about the extent to which the company has been able to monetize that.

So it's become sort of an interesting component within the company, but there's no take off of revenue generation.

Just as an example, the digital media unit in that company showed 11.6% growth in Q3. It had 11.3% in Q2. And then that growth had peaked in the most recent cycle at 12.6% back in 2023. So you're looking over the course of a few years. You see steady, relatively impressive growth, but nothing like Nvidia (NVDA) or even in what being is being promised for Oracle.

So it's just not as inviting a situation for investors who I think at this point are looking for home runs in the AI space.

RS: And what is happening in the AI tech space? We also saw Broadcom (AVGO) report. What would you say is the net effect across the board on, you mentioned NVIDIA, but the net effect on AI stocks and tech stocks in general, what's the general consensus, or what's part of the general consensus right now?

BS: You mentioned Broadcom, and I think that's an interesting stock to bring into it. So they reported last Friday, surged 9% following its earnings.

They beat expectations as well. Also announced new large customer, $10,000,000,000 customer for their AI chips, customized AI chips. There's a lot of speculation that customer is OpenAI, though neither company has confirmed that.

I think if you look at Oracle, which got a big pop based on contracts that it announced, people ignoring its recent earnings and looking more towards those customer acquisitions.

Meanwhile, you see Nebius (NBIS), they popped 49% on a deal that they signed with Microsoft (MSFT). That one, they're providing AI infrastructure at 17,400,000,000 over a five year span. So that's another company that got a huge surge just based on a customer acquisition.

You see the forward looking, it's not even in the guidance anymore, it's in these contract wins that the companies are getting. Broadcom also received a pop after the Oracle news, so there was just sort of a general AI pop there.

This was on Wednesday, Oracle rose following its earnings, and you saw Broadcom rise about 9% at the same time. You also saw Nvidia up about 4% on that day, and Palantir (PLTR) was up about 3%.

I think you're getting a small cadre of what people are considering the central AI growth stocks, and those are starting to react to each other as if they were a single sector.

RS: And as long as we're generally in the earnings conversation, Lululemon (LULU). I like to be a little bit affected when I say their name. They had earnings, and they saw a drop. What are the reasons, would you say, tariff based? What's happening over there?

BS: We didn't get to talk about it last week, but they reported on Friday and dropped 19% following their earnings.

They cut guidance basically based on tariffs. They pointed out that the elimination of the de minimis tariff exemption really hit their business hard. Analysts saying that this wasn't really baked into the stock.

The company also was pretty blunt about assessing their own situation. They called their product offering stale. So I think as a single stock story, they're taking a broad look at their offerings and the pricings and reassessing.

So there's a real turnaround situation trying to be put in play there. I think if you pull the lens back a little bit, and look at retailing in general, we saw some previous retail stocks get hit pretty hard on tariff related concerns.

You do have a situation now where these individual stocks are starting to feel the pinch of the new, international regime, and there's definitely gonna be winners and losers in the retail space from that.

RS: For stocks like Lululemon, on the higher price of things, are those more affected? We've been talking about these low cost retailers and how they're doing in the face of tariffs, in the face of economic hardship. What would you say to that point?

BS: I don't know if higher end is gonna feel the pinch because it's more expensive. We saw this play out with Dollar Tree (DLTR) was down sharply after its earnings largely because it's selling things so cheap that a relatively small increase dollar wise in the cost of what it's buying forces it to do a relatively high percentage change in the pricing.

So it's really challenging Dollar Tree's entire idea of itself, its business plan is based on getting the stuff extremely cheap. And so if that becomes 30%, 40%, 50% more expensive, it really matters to Dollar Tree a lot.

Meanwhile, we've seen some higher end retailers. Macy's (M) said their Bloomingdale's segment has done really well. And so that might be a consumer base that is immune to some of the shocks that we're seeing.

It could also be that because the items are already so high margin that the companies can afford to take a little bite and not really feel the pinch from that. So I think it's gonna be a little bit more complicated. I don't think there's gonna be a one to one situation.

I think investors, if you're looking into a retail stock, I think it's makes sense to look under the hood a little bit or try and find an ETF so that you're a little bit diversified as you you dip into the market.

RS: As investors, as participants in the market and in life, we really need to be thinking through things. They are not as obvious, to your point, it's not so simple, when the economy's bad, there's not necessarily a one to one correlation from sector to economy, from data to results. All these things, they must be picked apart.

BS: I think that's a good moral just for the current market. Disturbing economic data. We just got the job revisions for the twelve months leading to March, and that was revised down by 911,000 jobs, which basically represented half the jobs we thought were created during that period.

So every report about the jobs market, not only shows weakness as we stand now, but suggests that it has been weaker than we had expected even all along. Meanwhile, inflation data remains stubbornly high, roughly around 3%.

It looks like the Fed, which is meeting next week, is probably just going to give up on 2% inflation as a goal, and just declare victory at 3% and start cutting rates.

But even while this is happening, the market keeps finding new highs.

So you see, like you're pointing out, you would think that this succession of red flags for the economy would push the market lower, but the opposite is happening because I think that people are starting to concentrate, like I said, in these high growth possibilities.

So you see people ignoring an Adobe, but moving into an Oracle because it just looks like a a promising growth opportunity. So it'll be interesting how this plays out as we get more economic information. Maybe the rate cuts will be enough to sort of stem the economic tide.

Inflation won't shoot up due to tariffs or the falling interest rates. But, definitely stagflation is more of a concern than it had been. It's more of a reality. I mean, we're fundamentally in a stagflationary environment now this isn't like early eighties, late seventies kind of stagflation, but it's a mini stagflation as it stands. And so the question is, how long is it gonna last and how bad it's gonna go?

RS: And do not underestimate the psychology of the markets. Do not underestimate that factor in thinking about things.

To this point of what's happening in the world and the economy really segues nicely into - the IPO market is waking up a little bit and Klarna had its debut to the point of people needing to pay a bit later down the line because of economic situations. That's what Klarna (KLAR) is focused on. Talk to us a little bit about that. They saw a nice rise post debut.

BS: Yeah. That's interesting. I hadn't even really thought of that angle that Klarna might be in a good position in a more challenging economic environment. Then again, it survives on arbitraging rates in a certain sense.

So it might also run into difficulty as you start to see an uptick in delinquencies and things like that, they might feel the pinch there.

RS: Like we said, it's complicated.

BS: It's complicated. It rose 15% in its debut but finished off its highs. It's now trading below the highs that it reached immediately after going public.

There's been a run of higher profile IPOs that have done well lately. Bullish (BLSH) and Figma (FIG) come to mind.

Also, if you take things in aggregate, there's a a Renaissance IPO ETF. The ticker there is (IPO). It's up 20% year to date. It's meant to give broad exposure to recent IPOs. It's up 20% year to date, just off a fifty two week high.

So you see a lot of interests in these IPOs. It's a little top heavy and you get these higher profile ones, they get a lot of attention and get a little pop once they come up. If you look at a list of the IPOs, it's much more company to company. It's not like every IPO is is a winner.

The smaller ones tend to - it matters on the individual stock. But broadly speaking, you're seeing an interest there. So, if you're looking for a bullish factor, there's clearly demand for at least certain types of stocks. So there is investor money waiting to come into the market, just waiting for the right opportunity.

RS: Speaking of opportunities, we were talking on Investing Experts this week about small caps. Anything to say there?

BS: Traditionally, the small caps are kind of the leading edge of the market. They go up faster when the market goes up and go down faster when the market goes down. So it's a good leading indicator.

Even if you're not going to invest in those small caps, you can watch the action there as an indication of, it's just a much more volatile market than the S&P 500 (SP500) or the Dow (DJI) or whatever.

I think that in a troubled economic situation, smaller cap companies are more vulnerable. A company like NVIDIA, growth might fall off, but you're not gonna worry about NVIDIA going to zero.

And there could be, say, a small cap AI stock that seems like a big growth candidate. But if it runs into funding trouble, that might be it for the company.

So there's always a risk when you move down the market cap scale. It's a much more volatile situation. But on the other side, there's a much bigger chance for giant wins.

If I was giving advice to investors, I'd say, again, if you're betting on individual stocks, you really wanna dig under the hood in these conditions. I really don't think this is a dartboard market where you can just throw a dart at a bunch of names and whatever you get is gonna work out.

I really think it's getting to the point where it's a stock picker's market or you're gonna make collective bets in ETFs or in the broad market.

RS: And, of course, all advice, hypothetical, not literal on this podcast

BS: Yeah. I meant that educationally, not as a recommendation.

RS: We're thinking things through. Anything else for this week? What should we be looking for in the week ahead? Obviously, the Fed decision. What else would you add to that?

BS: Fed decision's obviously going to be the the central aspect. There was a 97% chance of a rate cut a week ago. Now that's 100% chance. And now the only question is whether it's going to be 25 basis points or 50 basis points. There's currently a 70% chance of a half point rate cut that was 0% a month ago.

So there's a feeling that the Fed might have missed its window a little bit and might have to catch up. You're also seeing an acceleration of rate cuts through the rest of the year.

So currently, there's an 80% chance that rates will be 75 basis points lower or more. By the end of the year, that chance was about fifty fifty a month ago. So you're looking for maybe three quarter point rate cuts at the three remaining meetings this year, or a half point cut this meeting or next meeting, and then a 25 basis point cut down the road a little bit.

So some combination in the next few meetings. That's what the market's baking in. There's also a 6% chance right now for a full percentage point lower by the end of the year.

That would be pretty dramatic, there'd be at least a half point rate cut and then two quarter point rate cut, something like that. So there's generally a feeling that's been growing in recent days that the Fed is gonna have to hit the gas on lowering interest rates.

Meanwhile, beyond the Fed meeting, there's not much in terms of earnings, but FedEx (FDX) is reporting next week. It's a good economic indicator stock that's central to a lot of companies' way they distribute their products, obviously, shipping packages.

And so even if you're not an investor or a potential investor in FedEx, it's always good to peek in on those earnings and see what it portends for the overall economy.

RS: In terms of the importance of the Fed meeting, it's getting a lot of press and, there's always talk about Fed meetings, but this one in particular, along with the previous one, feels very momentous.

What would you say as a fact man, as a newsman, what would you say in terms of contextualizing the importance of this Fed meeting?

BS: It has the the makings of a wild one, potentially. You still have the Lisa Cook debate, however you wanna frame that, going on. So it's not even clear at this moment whether she'll be a participant or not.

That kind of legal wrangling is ongoing. Whatever the decision is that the Fed makes at the meeting, you're likely gonna get dissenting votes. So it's gonna be a contentious meeting. It's gonna be a meeting with a lot of controversy.

There's gonna be eyes on what Trump's response to whatever the decision is going to be. There's also the usual kind of signaling that comes from the Fed either from their statement or from Powell's press conference.

So just in terms of possibilities, outcomes, this isn't your typical sleepy Fed meeting where everything's kinda wrapped up going into it and everyone's parsing placement of commas and things like that.

I think there'll be some actual news coming out of this meeting. So I agree with your assessment. This is going to be one of the most interesting meetings in a long time. I mean, probably since COVID calmed down.

We'll see what the commentary is vis a vis inflation and the economy. Because it's like I said, we are at mini stagflation right now, which is a kind of worst case economic scenario. So hearing Powell's recipe for getting out of that will be interesting to hear.

RS: We will obviously have coverage of the Fed meeting on Wall Street Breakfast, Wall Street Lunch. We're also going to have a a few dedicated episodes on Investing Experts about the Fed meeting.

Happy Friday, everybody. Happy Friday, Brian. Happy weekend, everybody. Don't forget to keep thinking things through, and don't forget to have real conversations with real people. Don't believe all the hyperbole on the Internet.

BS: Good advice.

RS: Talk it out.

data.title: Synopsys: Hurt By Design Issues
data.description:
I last took a look at shares of Synopsys, Inc. (NASDAQ:SNPS) in the spring of 2024, when I concluded that the company was selling assets on the cheap ahead of the Ansys deal closing.

At the time, the company sold its software integrity business in a $2 billion deal to pay for a $35 billion deal to acquire Ansys, as the divestment appeared to be taking place at cheap multiples. Such a cheap divestment ahead of a long and uncertain regulatory approval process provided added arguments to the cautious outlook for the shares.

With the deal finally taking place now, having closed over the summer, Synopsys sees a real slowdown, driven by market developments, but certainly also execution issues. While a violent pullback might be interesting, fundamental valuation support is still not yet seen.

Shedding Perspective

Early in 2024, Synopsys announced the purchase of Ansys to create a stronger, larger, and more diversified Electronic Design Automation market leader.

The company focuses on electronic design automation as well as design IP, both critical to accelerating electronics innovation, which stands at the forefront of foundry, semiconductor and electric system markets. This is the first step in the process, which eventually facilitates the rise of megatrends such as datacenters, networking, automotive, 5G, industrial automation, and others.

Over the past five years through 2023, revenues have risen some 70% to nearly $6 billion, with the company posting operating profits near $1.3 billion, resulting in GAAP earnings of $8 per share in 2023. Adjusted earnings came in at $11 per share, yet the vast majority of the $3 difference stems from stock-based compensation expenses, which I am not happy to adjust for.

A $550 stock was valued at $85 billion at the time, equal to 14 times sales and a very demanding earnings multiple at around 70 times GAAP earnings. All this changed as the company announced a $35 billion deal to acquire Ansys, to be paid roughly 50/50 between cash and stock. The transaction was designed to add silicon system design solutions capabilities to the business, creating a more holistic offering for its customers. The deal would add some $2.2 billion in sales, suggesting that a premium 15 times sales multiple has been paid.
